Coal: Coal has been the dominant global source of power for electricity generation since the age of electricity began. It remains the biggest single fuel type even today, generating over 10,000 TWh of electricity per year, with a 34.1% global share as of 2024 . Over half of total coal generation is in China, with India and USA being the other two top consumers. While coal’s share of electricity production has been in decline since 2013, the total TWh generated has continued rising in most years.
Gas: Natural gas (also increasingly referred to as fossil gas) is the third largest source of energy in the world, making up roughly 23% of total primary energy demand. It is around 95% methane (CH4) with small amounts of other gases present as well. While the uses for natural gas vary by region, it is used in similar proportions for generating electricity, heating buildings, and industrial applications. The global use of natural gas has been increasing for much of the past two decades (IEA), driven largely by a revolution in hydraulic fracturing which opened vast new reserves and lowered prices. Statistics presented here are focused on the role of natural gas in electricity generation.
Nuclear: Nuclear fission is a low-carbon but non-renewable source of energy. Fission power is used to boil water to generate steam, which then drives a turbine to generate electricity. Nuclear power plants are expensive to build and maintain, but generate no greenhouse gas emissions or other pollutants.
Hydro: Hydro power is the largest source of renewable energy in the world, and has been used to generate electricity for over a century. Hydro plants are also the most flexible source of renewable generation, since their reservoirs can be filled up during periods of low demand and released during periods of high demand. Hydro generation has largely remained flat over the past decade, with stable generation and a slowly declining share of overall electricity generation.
Solar: Solar power is the fastest growing source of energy in the world, thanks to falling costs and increasing efficiency. Solar is now the second largest source of renewable energy in the world, and is growing rapidly in many countries. Since solar power is intermittent, it needs to be paired with either other sources of generation or storage to provide a reliable source of energy.
Wind: Wind power is the third largest source of renewable energy in the world, and is growing rapidly in many countries. Wind power is intermittent, so it needs to be paired with either other sources of generation or storage to provide a reliable source of energy.
